expanded_description: Conditional statements are the building blocks of logical reasoning in geometry. An if-then statement expresses a hypothesis and a conclusion. Related statements—the converse, inverse, and contrapositive—are formed by negating or switching these parts. Understanding the truth relationships among these forms is essential for constructing valid proofs and analyzing geometric arguments.

core_concept.principle: A conditional statement has the form 'If p, then q' where p is the hypothesis and q is the conclusion. Three related conditionals are formed by switching or negating p and q: the converse (If q, then p), the inverse (If not p, then not q), and the contrapositive (If not q, then not p). A conditional and its contrapositive always have the same truth value, while the converse and inverse share the same truth value as each other.
core_concept.explanation: Think of a conditional statement as a promise. 'If it rains, then I will bring an umbrella.' The promise is only broken if it rains and you don't bring an umbrella. The converse would be 'If I bring an umbrella, then it rains'—a very different statement that isn't necessarily true. The contrapositive is 'If I don't bring an umbrella, then it isn't raining'—which is logically equivalent to the original promise. Understanding these relationships helps you avoid flawed reasoning and construct valid geometric proofs.

image.alt: A visual diagram showing the four related conditional statements arranged in a square. Top: Conditional (p → q). Right: Converse (q → p). Bottom: Contrapositive (¬q → ¬p). Left: Inverse (¬p → ¬q). Diagonal arrows show logical equivalence between conditional/contrapositive and converse/inverse.
image.caption: The four related conditionals: Conditional and contrapositive are logically equivalent. Converse and inverse are logically equivalent.

sections.introduction: A conditional statement, also called an implication, is a logical statement composed of two parts: a hypothesis (the 'if' part) and a conclusion (the 'then' part). Conditional statements are the language of geometric theorems—nearly every theorem can be written in if-then form.

introduction_image.alt: A conditional statement broken into parts: 'If [hypothesis], then [conclusion].' Example: 'If a figure is a square, then it has four right angles.' The hypothesis 'a figure is a square' is highlighted. The conclusion 'it has four right angles' is highlighted. Notation 'p → q' shown.
introduction_image.caption: Conditional Statement: 'If p, then q.' p is the hypothesis; q is the conclusion.

key_concept.definition: A conditional statement is written 'If p, then q,' denoted symbolically as p → q. p is the hypothesis (the condition), and q is the conclusion (the result). The statement is false only when p is true and q is false. In all other cases—p false (whether q is true or false) or both p and q true—the conditional is considered true.
key_concept.context: Geometric theorems are conditional statements. For example: 'If a triangle is isosceles, then its base angles are congruent.' The hypothesis is 'a triangle is isosceles' and the conclusion is 'its base angles are congruent.' Understanding conditionals is essential for reading, writing, and proving theorems.

image.alt: A truth table for p → q: p=T, q=T → T; p=T, q=F → F; p=F, q=T → T; p=F, q=F → T. Below: geometric example with diagram — 'If a point is on the perpendicular bisector, then it is equidistant from the endpoints.' Hypotenuse and conclusion labeled on the diagram.
image.caption: A conditional is false ONLY when the hypothesis is true and the conclusion is false.

process_flow.description: Read the conditional statement and determine which part is the hypothesis (following 'if') and which is the conclusion (following 'then').
process_flow.mechanism: Look for the words 'if' and 'then.' The hypothesis is the condition that must be met. The conclusion is the result that follows. Some statements omit 'then' but the logical structure remains. Symbolically: p = hypothesis, q = conclusion.
process_flow.result: Clear identification of p (hypothesis) and q (conclusion).
applications: 'If it is raining, then the ground is wet.' p = 'it is raining', q = 'the ground is wet.'
'If a triangle has two equal sides, then it is isosceles.' p = 'a triangle has two equal sides', q = 'it is isosceles.'
'All squares are rectangles.' Rewrite: 'If a figure is a square, then it is a rectangle.' p = 'a figure is a square', q = 'it is a rectangle.'

image.alt: A conditional statement with the hypothesis and conclusion labeled. A geometric diagram illustrating the statement: an isosceles triangle with equal sides marked, base angles marked equal. The hypothesis and conclusion are connected to the diagram elements.
image.caption: Step 1: Identify p (hypothesis) and q (conclusion) in the conditional statement.

process_flow.description: Evaluate whether the conditional statement is true or false. It is false only when p is true and q is false simultaneously.
process_flow.mechanism: Check: Can you find a case where the hypothesis is satisfied but the conclusion does NOT hold? If yes, the conditional is false. If no such case exists, the conditional is true. Symbolically, p → q is equivalent to 'not p or q' — the only false row is (p true, q false).
process_flow.result: A determination of true or false for the conditional statement.
applications: 'If x > 5, then x > 3.' True — any number >5 is automatically >3. No counterexample exists.
'If a number is prime, then it is odd.' False — counterexample: 2 is prime but not odd.
'If a quadrilateral is a square, then it has four right angles.' True — all squares have four right angles.

image.alt: Truth table for p → q highlighted. A visual showing a counterexample: a rhombus (hypothesis true: four equal sides? No, this is for square...) — actually showing a prime number 2 as counterexample to 'prime → odd'. The false row of the truth table is circled.
image.caption: Step 2: A conditional is false only when p is true and q is false. Find a counterexample to prove falsity.

process_flow.process: Rewrite in If-Then Form When Necessary
process_flow.description: Many geometric statements are not written as conditionals but can be rewritten into if-then form by identifying the hypothesis and conclusion.
process_flow.mechanism: Look for keywords: 'all,' 'every,' 'each' often indicate the hypothesis. 'All squares are rectangles' becomes 'If a figure is a square, then it is a rectangle.' 'Vertical angles are congruent' becomes 'If two angles are vertical angles, then they are congruent.'
process_flow.result: The statement expressed in standard if-then form, ready for logical analysis.
applications: 'Every equilateral triangle is equiangular.' → 'If a triangle is equilateral, then it is equiangular.'
'Supplementary angles sum to 180°.' → 'If two angles are supplementary, then their measures sum to 180°.'
'Points on the perpendicular bisector are equidistant from the endpoints.' → 'If a point lies on the perpendicular bisector of a segment, then it is equidistant from the endpoints.'

image.alt: Several geometric statements listed on the left: 'All squares are rectangles,' 'Vertical angles are congruent,' 'Supplementary angles sum to 180°.' Arrows point to their if-then forms on the right. Each rewritten form has p and q labeled.
image.caption: Step 3: Rewrite geometric statements in if-then form for logical analysis.

classifications.value: The truth table defines exactly when a conditional statement is true or false.
characteristics: p = True, q = True → p → q is TRUE (promise kept)
p = True, q = False → p → q is FALSE (promise broken — this is the ONLY false case)
p = False, q = True → p → q is TRUE (vacuously true — condition wasn't met, so no promise broken)
p = False, q = False → p → q is TRUE (vacuously true)
classifications.behavior: The conditional is 'vacuously true' when the hypothesis is false—regardless of the conclusion. This is a subtle but important logical point. 'If pigs can fly, then I am the president' is logically true (since the hypothesis is false), even though it seems absurd.

examples.explanation:
'If a triangle has four sides, then its angle sum is 1000°.'

Hypothesis: 'a triangle has four sides' is ALWAYS false (triangles have 3 sides).

Since the hypothesis is never true, there is no case where p is true and q is false.

Therefore, the conditional is vacuously TRUE. This is logically correct, even though the statement describes an impossible situation.

image.alt: Complete truth table for p → q with four rows. The row (p=T, q=F) is highlighted in red as the only false case. A geometric example with a false hypothesis shown as 'vacuously true.'
image.caption: The conditional is false only when p is true and q is false. All other rows are true.

image.alt: Overview diagram: Structure of a conditional (p → q), truth table, geometric example with diagram, and rewritten forms of common geometric statements. Clean, organized layout.
image.caption: Conditional Statements: structure, truth table, and rewriting geometric statements in if-then form.

sections.introduction: From any conditional statement, three related statements can be formed by switching or negating the hypothesis and conclusion. The converse switches them. The inverse negates both. The contrapositive does both—switches and negates. Each has its own truth value, independent of the original conditional.

introduction_image.alt: The four related conditionals displayed in a 2×2 grid: Original (p→q) top-left, Converse (q→p) top-right, Inverse (¬p→¬q) bottom-left, Contrapositive (¬q→¬p) bottom-right. Each written in words and symbols. Arrows showing relationships.
introduction_image.caption: Four related conditionals: original, converse, inverse, and contrapositive.

key_concept.definition: Given the conditional 'If p, then q' (p → q): The CONVERSE switches hypothesis and conclusion: 'If q, then p' (q → p). The INVERSE negates both: 'If not p, then not q' (¬p → ¬q). The CONTRAPOSITIVE switches and negates both: 'If not q, then not p' (¬q → ¬p). The original conditional and its contrapositive are logically equivalent (always have the same truth value). The converse and inverse are logically equivalent to each other.
key_concept.context: In geometry, confusing a theorem with its converse is a common error. 'If a triangle is isosceles, then its base angles are congruent' is true. Its converse 'If base angles are congruent, then the triangle is isosceles' is also true—but this is a separate theorem that requires its own proof. Not all converses are true.

image.alt: A clear diagram: Original 'If it is a square, then it is a rectangle' (TRUE). Converse 'If it is a rectangle, then it is a square' (FALSE — counterexample: non-square rectangle). Inverse 'If it is not a square, then it is not a rectangle' (FALSE). Contrapositive 'If it is not a rectangle, then it is not a square' (TRUE). Truth values and counterexamples shown.
image.caption: A true conditional may have a false converse and inverse. The contrapositive is always equivalent.

classifications.value: Formed by switching the hypothesis and conclusion of the original conditional.
characteristics: Original: p → q ('If p, then q')
Converse: q → p ('If q, then p')
The converse is NOT logically equivalent to the original—it may have a different truth value
A conditional and its converse are both true exactly when p and q are logically equivalent (p ↔ q)
classifications.behavior: The converse reverses the direction of implication. The truth of the original does NOT guarantee the truth of the converse. Each must be verified independently. The converse of a true theorem may be false or may itself be a theorem (requiring separate proof).

examples.process: Original vs. Converse of a geometric statement
examples.explanation:
Original: 'If a quadrilateral is a square, then it has four right angles.' (TRUE — all squares have 4 right angles.)

Converse: 'If a quadrilateral has four right angles, then it is a square.' (FALSE — a rectangle has 4 right angles but is not necessarily a square.)

The original is true but its converse is false. They are NOT equivalent.

image.alt: Left: A square (hypothesis true, conclusion true — conditional holds). Right: A non-square rectangle (hypothesis of converse true: has 4 right angles, but conclusion false: not a square). The rectangle is a counterexample to the converse.
image.caption: Original: Square → 4 right angles (TRUE). Converse: 4 right angles → Square (FALSE — rectangle counterexample).

classifications.value: Formed by negating both the hypothesis and conclusion of the original conditional.
characteristics: Original: p → q
Inverse: ¬p → ¬q ('If not p, then not q')
The inverse is logically equivalent to the CONVERSE, not to the original
To negate a statement, insert 'not' or use the opposite property
classifications.behavior: The inverse negates both parts. Like the converse, it is not logically equivalent to the original. The inverse of a true theorem may be false. The inverse is true exactly when the converse is true.

examples.explanation:
Original: 'If it is a square, then it has four right angles.' (TRUE)

Inverse: 'If it is NOT a square, then it does NOT have four right angles.' (FALSE — a rectangle is not a square but DOES have four right angles.)

The inverse is false for the same reason the converse is false—they are logically equivalent.

image.alt: Left: A square. Right: A non-square rectangle. The rectangle satisfies ¬p (not a square) but violates ¬q (it DOES have four right angles). The rectangle is a counterexample to the inverse.
image.caption: Inverse: Not a square → Not 4 right angles (FALSE — rectangle is a counterexample).

classifications.value: Formed by switching AND negating both the hypothesis and conclusion.
characteristics: Original: p → q
Contrapositive: ¬q → ¬p ('If not q, then not p')
The contrapositive IS logically equivalent to the original conditional
If the original is true, the contrapositive is guaranteed true. If the original is false, the contrapositive is false.
classifications.behavior: The contrapositive is a powerful tool in proofs. When a statement is difficult to prove directly, proving its contrapositive is often easier—and because they are equivalent, proving one proves the other.

examples.explanation:
Original: 'If a quadrilateral is a square, then it has four right angles.' (TRUE)

Contrapositive: 'If a quadrilateral does NOT have four right angles, then it is NOT a square.' (TRUE — any shape lacking 4 right angles cannot be a square.)

The original and contrapositive are both true. They are logically equivalent statements.

image.alt: A rhombus with 60° and 120° angles (not 4 right angles). Contrapositive: 'No 4 right angles → Not a square.' The rhombus satisfies ¬q (no 4 right angles) and indeed ¬p (not a square). Statement holds.
image.caption: Contrapositive: No 4 right angles → Not a square (TRUE — equivalent to original).

image.alt: A comprehensive 2×2 table: Original (p→q), Converse (q→p), Inverse (¬p→¬q), Contrapositive (¬q→¬p). Each with definition, example, and truth value. Equivalence arrows: Original ↔ Contrapositive, Converse ↔ Inverse.
image.caption: The four related conditionals: definitions, logical equivalences, and truth relationships.

sections.introduction: Logical equivalence means two statements always have the same truth value. The conditional and its contrapositive are logically equivalent, as are the converse and inverse. Understanding these equivalences is crucial for constructing valid proofs and avoiding logical fallacies.

introduction_image.alt: Two pairs of statements connected by '≡' symbols: p→q ≡ ¬q→¬p (conditional and contrapositive). q→p ≡ ¬p→¬q (converse and inverse). Truth tables for each pair shown side by side confirming identical truth values.
introduction_image.caption: Logical equivalences: p→q ≡ ¬q→¬p. q→p ≡ ¬p→¬q. Truth tables confirm these pairs match.

key_concept.definition: Two statements are logically equivalent if they have the same truth value in every possible case. The conditional p → q and its contrapositive ¬q → ¬p are logically equivalent. The converse q → p and inverse ¬p → ¬q are logically equivalent. These equivalences allow proof by contrapositive: proving ¬q → ¬p is a valid way to prove p → q.
key_concept.context: Proof by contrapositive is a standard technique. If a direct proof of 'If p, then q' is difficult, prove 'If not q, then not p' instead. Since these are equivalent, establishing the contrapositive automatically establishes the original statement.

image.alt: Two truth tables side by side: p→q and ¬q→¬p. Both have identical T/F patterns (T, F, T, T). Below: A geometric example — 'If a triangle is equilateral, then it is isosceles.' Contrapositive: 'If a triangle is not isosceles, then it is not equilateral.' Both true. Proof strategy diagram.
image.caption: Logical equivalence: same truth table pattern means the statements are interchangeable in proofs.

classifications.value: A proof strategy that proves the contrapositive instead of the original conditional, relying on their logical equivalence.
characteristics: To prove p → q, assume ¬q and derive ¬p
Equivalent to the original statement—if the contrapositive is proven, the original is proven
Often easier than direct proof, especially when the conclusion involves negation
Widely used in geometry and all branches of mathematics
classifications.behavior: Proof by contrapositive starts with 'Assume the conclusion is false' and shows the hypothesis must also be false. This is logically sound because the contrapositive is equivalent to the original.

examples.process: Using contrapositive in a geometric proof
examples.explanation:
Theorem: 'If a triangle has two equal angles, then it has two equal sides.'

Direct proof may require construction. Contrapositive approach:

Contrapositive: 'If a triangle does NOT have two equal sides, then it does NOT have two equal angles.'

Assume all three sides are different (scalene). Prove all three angles are different.

If the contrapositive is proven true, the original theorem is automatically true.

image.alt: A flowchart showing the contrapositive proof strategy: Original: p → q (target). Contrapositive: ¬q → ¬p (proven instead). Because they are equivalent, the original is proven. A scalene triangle diagram with different angles illustrates the contrapositive.
image.caption: Proof by contrapositive: prove ¬q → ¬p, and p → q follows by logical equivalence.

classifications.value: When both a conditional and its converse are true, the statements are logically equivalent and can be combined into a biconditional: 'p if and only if q' (p ↔ q).
characteristics: p ↔ q means p → q AND q → p are both true
Read as 'p if and only if q' or 'p iff q'
p and q are logically equivalent—they imply each other
Many geometric definitions are biconditionals
classifications.behavior: Biconditionals express complete logical equivalence. A definition like 'A triangle is equilateral if and only if it is equiangular' means both the conditional and its converse are true theorems.

examples.process: Biconditional from a true conditional and true converse
examples.explanation:
Conditional: 'If a triangle is equilateral, then it is equiangular.' (TRUE)

Converse: 'If a triangle is equiangular, then it is equilateral.' (TRUE)

Since both are true, they combine: 'A triangle is equilateral IF AND ONLY IF it is equiangular.'

This biconditional is a powerful theorem—the two properties are equivalent in triangles.

image.alt: An equilateral/equiangular triangle. Two arrows: Equilateral → Equiangular (true). Equiangular → Equilateral (true). Both combine into: 'Equilateral ↔ Equiangular.' The biconditional symbol and if-and-only-if wording shown.
image.caption: Biconditional: both directions are true, so the properties are equivalent.

worked_examples.title: Analyzing Related Conditionals
worked_examples.problem:
Consider the statement: 'If a quadrilateral is a rhombus, then its diagonals are perpendicular.'

1) Write the converse, inverse, and contrapositive.

2) Determine the truth value of each.

3) Are the conditional and its converse both true? If so, write a biconditional.

step_by_step.explanation:
Original (p → q): If a quad is a rhombus, then its diagonals are perpendicular.

Converse (q → p): If a quad's diagonals are perpendicular, then it is a rhombus.

Inverse (¬p → ¬q): If a quad is NOT a rhombus, then its diagonals are NOT perpendicular.

Contrapositive (¬q → ¬p): If a quad's diagonals are NOT perpendicular, then it is NOT a rhombus.

Original: FALSE. A rhombus does NOT necessarily have perpendicular diagonals. Counterexample: a rhombus that is not a kite (or more precisely, a rhombus with angles not 90° has non-perpendicular diagonals? Actually, all rhombuses DO have perpendicular diagonals. Wait — that's true for a rhombus! Let's recheck.)

Correction: In a rhombus, diagonals ARE always perpendicular. So the original is TRUE.

Converse: FALSE. A kite has perpendicular diagonals but is not necessarily a rhombus. Counterexample: a kite.

Inverse: FALSE (equivalent to converse).

Contrapositive: TRUE (equivalent to original).

Original is TRUE, but converse is FALSE. Therefore, the properties are NOT equivalent.

'Rhombus' and 'perpendicular diagonals' do NOT imply each other mutually.

A biconditional is NOT possible here. A rhombus guarantees perpendicular diagonals, but perpendicular diagonals do NOT guarantee a rhombus (could be a kite).

image.alt: Four diagrams: (1) Rhombus with perpendicular diagonals — original TRUE. (2) Kite with perpendicular diagonals — counterexample to converse. (3) Non-rhombus kite — counterexample to inverse. (4) Quad with non-perpendicular diagonals — not a rhombus, contrapositive TRUE.
image.caption: Truth analysis: original true, converse false, inverse false, contrapositive true.

worked_examples.final_answer:
Original: TRUE (rhombus → perpendicular diagonals).

Converse: FALSE (perpendicular diagonals ⇏ rhombus; kite is counterexample).

Inverse: FALSE (not rhombus ⇏ not perpendicular; kite is counterexample).

Contrapositive: TRUE (not perpendicular → not rhombus).

Biconditional: Not possible — conditional true but converse false.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Original ↔ Contrapositive (same truth value). Converse ↔ Inverse (same truth value). A biconditional requires BOTH original and converse to be true.

sections.introduction: Logical reasoning with conditionals has several common pitfalls. The most frequent errors involve confusing a conditional with its converse, assuming the inverse follows from the original, and misapplying the negation of a statement.

key_concept.definition: Common errors include: assuming the converse is true because the original is true (fallacy of the converse), assuming the inverse is true because the original is true (fallacy of the inverse), incorrectly negating a statement (especially with quantifiers like 'all' or 'some'), and confusing the contrapositive with the converse or inverse.
key_concept.context: These logical fallacies appear in everyday reasoning and in flawed geometric proofs. Learning to recognize them prevents invalid arguments and strengthens proof-writing skills.

image.alt: Four warning panels: (1) 'If it's a dog, then it's an animal' — Converse 'If it's an animal, then it's a dog' is FALSE (cat counterexample). (2) Negation errors: 'not all are' vs. 'all are not.' (3) Confusing contrapositive with converse. (4) Forgetting vacuous truth.
image.caption: Common conditional errors: assuming converse/inverse are true, negating incorrectly, and confusing the forms.

classifications.value: The most common logical error: 'If p, then q is true, so if q, then p must also be true.'
characteristics: Error: 'All squares are rectangles, so all rectangles are squares.'
The truth of p → q says nothing about q → p
The converse requires its own independent verification
Many famous false conjectures are converses of true theorems
classifications.behavior: This fallacy reverses the logical flow. Just because every p is a q does not mean every q is a p. The set of p's may be a proper subset of the set of q's.

examples.process: Fallacy of the converse in geometry
examples.explanation:
True theorem: 'If a triangle is equilateral, then it is isosceles.'

False converse: 'If a triangle is isosceles, then it is equilateral.'

A triangle with sides 5, 5, 3 is isosceles but NOT equilateral. The converse is false.

Every equilateral triangle is isosceles, but not every isosceles triangle is equilateral.

image.alt: Venn diagram: Equilateral triangles as a smaller circle inside the larger Isosceles circle. Caption: 'All equilateral triangles are isosceles (True). But not all isosceles triangles are equilateral (Converse False).' An isosceles triangle with sides 5,5,3 shown.
image.caption: Equilateral ⊂ Isosceles. The converse incorrectly assumes the sets are equal.

classifications.note: Solution: Never assume the converse. After proving p → q, ask: 'Is q → p also true?' Test it independently. Look for counterexamples where q holds but p does not.

classifications.value: Errors in forming the negation of a statement, especially when quantifiers like 'all,' 'some,' or 'none' are involved.
characteristics: Error: The negation of 'All p are q' is 'No p are q' (WRONG — the correct negation is 'Some p are not q')
Error: The negation of 'x > 5' is 'x < 5' (WRONG — the correct negation is 'x ≤ 5')
Correct: Negation means the opposite truth value, not the opposite extreme
classifications.behavior: Logical negation is precise. The negation of 'All A are B' is 'There exists at least one A that is not B.' The negation of 'x = 5' is 'x ≠ 5.' Pay careful attention to inequalities and quantifiers.

examples.explanation:
Statement: 'All triangles have 180° angle sum.'

Negation: 'There exists a triangle whose angle sum is NOT 180°.' (Not: 'No triangle has 180° angle sum.')

Statement: 'x is greater than 10.'

Negation: 'x ≤ 10.' (Not: 'x < 10.')

Statement: 'The quadrilateral is a square.'

Negation: 'The quadrilateral is NOT a square.' (Could be rectangle, rhombus, trapezoid, etc.)

image.alt: Two columns: 'Incorrect Negation' and 'Correct Negation.' Examples: 'All A are B' → Incorrect: 'No A are B.' Correct: 'Some A are not B.' 'x > 5' → Incorrect: 'x < 5.' Correct: 'x ≤ 5.' Visual Venn diagrams illustrate each.
image.caption: Correct negation: the logical opposite, not the extreme opposite.

classifications.note: Solution: To negate 'All p are q,' say 'There exists a p that is not q.' To negate an inequality, include the equality case. To negate a simple statement, insert or remove 'not.'

classifications.type: Confusing Contrapositive with Converse or Inverse
classifications.value: Mixing up the operations—which form switches, which negates, and which does both.
characteristics: Converse: SWITCH only (p→q becomes q→p)
Inverse: NEGATE only (p→q becomes ¬p→¬q)
Contrapositive: SWITCH AND NEGATE (p→q becomes ¬q→¬p)
The contrapositive is equivalent; the converse and inverse are not
classifications.behavior: A memory trick: 'Contrapositive' has 'contra' (opposite) and 'positive' (position) — it switches positions AND takes opposites. The converse only switches; the inverse only opposes.

examples.process: Memory aid for the four forms
examples.explanation:
Original: If p, then q. (p→q)

CONverse: CONtains the switch — p and q change places. (q→p)

INverse: INserts 'not' — negate both. (¬p→¬q)

CONTRAPOSITIVE: CONTRAry (negate) + POSITIVE (position switch) — do both. (¬q→¬p)

Only the contrapositive is equivalent to the original.

image.alt: A 2×2 table: Rows: Switch? (No/Yes). Columns: Negate? (No/Yes). Cells: Original (no switch, no negate), Converse (switch, no negate), Inverse (no switch, negate), Contrapositive (switch, negate). Equivalence arrows shown.
image.caption: Decision table: Switch changes order; Negate adds 'not.' Contrapositive does both.

classifications.note: Solution: Use the 2×2 mental table. Switch? = exchange p and q. Negate? = add ¬ to both. Contrapositive = both operations. Only contrapositive ≡ original.

worked_examples.title: Identifying Conditional Statement Errors
worked_examples.problem:
A student makes these claims. Identify and correct the logical error in each.

1) 'If a figure is a square, then it is a rectangle' is true, so 'If a figure is a rectangle, then it is a square' must also be true.

2) The inverse of 'If x > 10, then x > 5' is 'If x < 10, then x < 5.'

3) 'All equilateral triangles are equiangular' means the same as 'All equiangular triangles are equilateral.'

step_by_step.explanation:
Error: Assuming the converse is true because the original is true. This is the fallacy of the converse.

Original (TRUE): Square → Rectangle. Every square is a rectangle.

Converse (FALSE): Rectangle → Square. A non-square rectangle is a counterexample.

The truth of the original guarantees nothing about the converse.

Error: 'x > 10' negated is 'x ≤ 10', not 'x < 10'.

Correct inverse: 'If x ≤ 10, then x ≤ 5.' (Wait — is this true? If x ≤ 10, x could be 7 which is >5. So the inverse is false. The original is true. This confirms inverse ≠ original.)

The student's error was in the negation itself: the opposite of > is ≤, not <.

Error: Assuming a statement and its converse say the same thing.

'All equilateral triangles are equiangular' = 'If equilateral, then equiangular.' (TRUE)

'All equiangular triangles are equilateral' = 'If equiangular, then equilateral.' This is the CONVERSE, which is also TRUE (in triangles), but it is a DIFFERENT statement that requires separate proof.

Even though both are true here, they are not logically equivalent statements—they just happen to both be true for triangles.

image.alt: Three panels: (1) Square→Rectangle (true), Rectangle→Square (false with non-square rectangle counterexample). (2) Number line showing negation of >10 is ≤10, not <10. (3) Two arrows between equilateral and equiangular — both true for triangles but separate theorems.
image.caption: Correcting logical errors: converse fallacy, negation precision, and distinctness of converse.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) Error: Assuming the converse is true. Rectangle ⇏ Square (counterexample: non-square rectangle).

2) Error: Negation of > is ≤, not <. Correct inverse: 'If x ≤ 10, then x ≤ 5.' (This is false; original is true.)

3) Error: Treating a statement and its converse as identical. They are separate claims, each requiring proof.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Converse is not equivalent to original. Correct negation preserves logical opposite. Converse and original are separate statements.

sections.introduction: Mastery of conditional statements requires practice identifying hypothesis and conclusion, writing related forms, determining truth values, and applying logical equivalence. Work through these problems to build fluency with the language of logical reasoning.

worked_examples.title: Conditional Statements Practice
worked_examples.problem:
1) Identify hypothesis and conclusion: 'If two angles are vertical, then they are congruent.'

2) Write the converse, inverse, and contrapositive of the statement in #1. Determine the truth value of each.

3) Rewrite 'All right angles are congruent' in if-then form. Write its contrapositive.

4) A conditional is true but its converse is false. Give an original geometric example not used in this section.

5) Write a biconditional: 'A triangle is equilateral if and only if it has three congruent sides.' Is this true by definition?

step_by_step.explanation:
p (hypothesis): 'Two angles are vertical.'

q (conclusion): 'They are congruent.'

p → q: If two angles are vertical, then they are congruent.

Original (p→q): If vertical, then congruent. TRUE (Vertical Angles Theorem).

Converse (q→p): If congruent, then vertical. FALSE (congruent angles can be anywhere, not necessarily vertical).

Inverse (¬p→¬q): If not vertical, then not congruent. FALSE (angles not vertical can still be congruent).

Contrapositive (¬q→¬p): If not congruent, then not vertical. TRUE (equivalent to original).

If-then form: 'If two angles are right angles, then they are congruent.'

Contrapositive: 'If two angles are NOT congruent, then they are NOT both right angles.'

(Both original and contrapositive are TRUE.)

Original: 'If a triangle is obtuse, then it has exactly one obtuse angle.' (TRUE — by definition, an obtuse triangle has one obtuse angle; it can't have more because the sum would exceed 180°.)

Converse: 'If a triangle has exactly one obtuse angle, then it is obtuse.' (Actually, this is also TRUE by definition... Let's find a better example.)

Better example — Original: 'If a quadrilateral is a trapezoid, then it has at least one pair of parallel sides.' (TRUE by definition.)

Converse: 'If a quadrilateral has at least one pair of parallel sides, then it is a trapezoid.' (FALSE — a parallelogram has TWO pairs of parallel sides and is not classified as a trapezoid in exclusive definition.)

'A triangle is equilateral if and only if it has three congruent sides.'

This is a biconditional: Equilateral ↔ Three congruent sides.

Is it true? YES — this is the DEFINITION of an equilateral triangle. Definitions are biconditionals by nature: a figure satisfies the definition if and only if it is the defined object.

image.alt: Five panels: (1) Vertical angles diagram. (2) Four related conditionals with truth values. (3) Right angles → congruent, and contrapositive. (4) Trapezoid/parallelogram example for true conditional, false converse. (5) Equilateral triangle ↔ three congruent sides.
image.caption: Conditional statements practice: identifying parts, writing forms, truth evaluation, and biconditionals.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) p: 'Two angles are vertical.' q: 'They are congruent.'

2) Original: TRUE. Converse: FALSE. Inverse: FALSE. Contrapositive: TRUE.

3) If-then: 'If two angles are right angles, then they are congruent.' Contrapositive: 'If two angles are NOT congruent, then they are NOT both right angles.'

4) Example: 'If a quad is a trapezoid, then it has one pair of parallel sides.' (TRUE) Converse: FALSE (parallelogram counterexample).

5) Yes, true by definition. Definitions are always biconditional.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Hypothesis/conclusion identification, related conditionals, truth values, logical equivalence, and biconditionals from definitions.

summary: Conditional statements are the logical foundation of geometric reasoning. Key points: 1) Structure: 'If p, then q' (p → q). p is the hypothesis; q is the conclusion. The conditional is false ONLY when p is true and q is false. 2) Related Forms: Converse (q → p) — switch. Inverse (¬p → ¬q) — negate both. Contrapositive (¬q → ¬p) — switch and negate both. 3) Logical Equivalence: The original conditional and its contrapositive are logically equivalent (always share the same truth value). The converse and inverse are logically equivalent to each other. Neither the converse nor inverse is equivalent to the original. 4) Biconditional (p ↔ q): 'p if and only if q.' True when both p → q and q → p are true. Geometric definitions are biconditionals. 5) Proof by Contrapositive: To prove p → q, prove its contrapositive ¬q → ¬p instead. They are equivalent, so proving one proves the other. 6) Common Errors: Assuming the converse is true (fallacy of the converse), incorrectly negating statements (especially with 'all' or inequalities), confusing the four related forms, and forgetting vacuous truth. 7) Geometric statements can be rewritten in if-then form for logical analysis, enabling precise reasoning about hypotheses, conclusions, and their relationships.

review_questions_image.alt: Concept map with 'Conditional Statements' in center. Branches: Structure (p→q, hypothesis, conclusion, truth table), Related Forms (converse q→p, inverse ¬p→¬q, contrapositive ¬q→¬p), Logical Equivalence (original ≡ contrapositive, converse ≡ inverse), Biconditional (p↔q when both directions true), Common Errors (converse fallacy, incorrect negation, form confusion).
review_questions_image.caption: Conditional Statements Review: structure, related forms, logical equivalence, and common errors.
